network.overall_strategy: The network centers valine synthesis on the best-supported prebiotic route: Strecker synthesis from isobutyraldehyde, ammonia, and hydrogen cyanide to the valine aminonitrile, followed by hydrolysis to racemic valine under mild to moderate aqueous conditions. To satisfy the literature, the network explicitly treats branched C4 precursor generation as the main bottleneck and therefore includes multiple low-selectivity, convergent feeder routes to isobutyraldehyde and alpha-ketoisovalerate rather than overstating any single selective origin. Hydrothermal alkaline-vent chemistry supplies reduced carbon hubs such as formate, acetaldehyde, and pyruvate under moderate vent conditions; surface environments generate/concentrate HCN, formamide, and support Strecker/Bucherer-Bergs chemistry in evaporitic or UV-exposed settings; biochemical/prebiotic settings complete hydrolysis, aminonitrile protection/deprotection, reductive amination alternatives, and peptide incorporation. Several pathways are intentionally marked as speculative where the literature identifies unresolved gaps, especially the abiotic formation of isobutyraldehyde and alpha-ketoisovalerate.

reactions.name: Greigite-catalyzed CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface electron transfer reduces CO2 to surface-bound formyl/formate equivalents on iron-sulfur phases.
reactions.reasoning: CO2 reduction to formate on Fe-S minerals is consistent with alkaline vent carbon fixation models and iron-sulfur world chemistry, where greigite and mackinawite act as prebiotic analogs of metallocofactors (Russell et al., 1994; Martin & Russell, 2007). Thermodynamic favorability for amino-acid precursor synthesis under reduced hydrothermal conditions is supported by Amend & Shock (1998).

reactions.name: Magnetite-mediated hydrothermal generation of acetaldehyde inventory from reduced C1/C2 carbon

conditions.other: subseafloor or alkaline-vent reducing conditions; low-selectivity FTT-like carbonyl generation

reactions.mechanism: Successive reduction and C-C bond-forming processes in Fischer-Tropsch-type hydrothermal chemistry generate low-molecular-weight oxygenates including acetaldehyde from reduced carbon pools.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal FTT chemistry on Fe-bearing minerals generates alcohols, hydrocarbons, and oxygenates that can seed aldehyde inventories, although product distributions are broad rather than selective (McCollom, Ritter, & Simoneit, 1999). This step is included as a low-selectivity feeder to acetaldehyde rather than as a selective route. Its thermodynamic plausibility in reduced hydrothermal systems is compatible with Amend & Shock (1998).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis on iron minerals

conditions.other: moderate alkaline vent regime; avoiding prolonged >240C exposure

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-catalyzed reductive carbon fixation and C-C bond formation furnish alpha-keto acid inventories including pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate-like alpha-keto acid generation in protometabolic and hydrothermal iron-mineral settings is supported conceptually by nonenzymatic carbon fixation and rTCA analog studies, though exact prebiotic pathways remain distributed rather than single-step (Muchowska, Varma, & Moran, 2019). The moderate alkaline-vent framing follows the literature preference against black-smoker destruction of amino acids (Miller & Bada, 1988; Martin & Russell, 2007).

reactions.name: Surface concentration of atmospheric/discharge HCN in evaporitic pools

conditions.other: UV-exposed tidal flats or warm little ponds; concentration by evaporation/freezing cycles

reactions.mechanism: Environmental accumulation node representing photochemical/atmospheric supply and local mineral-assisted concentration/persistence of HCN in surface settings.
reactions.reasoning: The research report emphasizes that HCN availability is environmental and concentration-limited rather than well represented by a simple synthetic step from CO2 and NH3. Oceanic HCN is low and hydrolyzes rapidly at high temperature, so concentrating surface settings are required (Miyakawa et al., 2002). TiO2-supported UV photochemistry and evaporitic concentration are plausible environmental aids, while atmospheric discharge production of cyanide is consistent with Miller-Urey-type scenarios (Miller, 1953; Johnson et al., 2008; Parker et al., 2011). This node is an explicitly environmental transfer/concentration step rather than an invented molecular synthesis.

reactions.name: HCN hydration and concentration to formamide in UV-exposed surface settings
reactions.mechanism: HCN-derived nitrile hydration and photochemically sustained surface chemistry generate formamide-rich microenvironments.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide is a recognized prebiotic intermediate in cyanide chemistry and UV-driven surface systems, and TiO2 photocatalysis promotes formamide-based prebiotic chemistry including nucleobase formation (Saladino et al., 2006). The report specifically highlights formamide-rich settings as beneficial for aminonitrile protection (Green, Xu, & Sutherland, 2021; 2023).

reactions.name: Low-selectivity branched aldehyde diversification to isobutyraldehyde from acetaldehyde-rich inventory

conditions.catalyst: sulfide minerals with UV-exposed drying-rewetting surfaces
conditions.other: evaporitic pools; low selectivity; branched products are minor among higher aldehydes

reactions.mechanism: Aldol/radical diversification of acetaldehyde-rich mixtures under mineral-assisted surface conditions yields a complex higher-aldehyde distribution including trace branched C4 aldehydes such as isobutyraldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The literature identifies isobutyraldehyde formation as the main unresolved bottleneck and specifically warns against representing it as a clean selective conversion. Preiner et al. (2023) showed metal-sulfide-catalyzed acetylene chemistry yielding acetaldehyde followed by aldol diversification to higher, including branched, aldehydes, but not selective isobutyraldehyde. Spark-discharge work also supports branched aldehydes arising in complex radical mixtures rather than by a single clean step (Parker et al., 2011; Keefe, Newton, & Miller, 1995). This reaction is therefore explicitly low-selectivity and bottlenecked.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of valine aminonitrile from isobutyraldehyde

conditions.other: mild aqueous solution near pH ~9, evaporitic pool or pond margin

reactions.mechanism: Isobutyraldehyde condenses with NH3 to an imine, followed by cyanide addition to form the alpha-aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the most experimentally validated route to valine. The operative aldehyde precursor for valine is isobutyraldehyde, and Strecker synthesis proceeds best near pH 9 where free NH3 is available; hydrolysis then drives the equilibrium forward (Miller & Van Trump, 1981; Keefe, Newton, & Miller, 1995; Devergne, Magrino, Pietrucci, & Saitta, 2021). The mineral setting here is used as a plausible surface buffer/concentrator rather than an unsupported alternative mechanism.

reactions.name: Formamide-mediated N-formylation of valine aminonitrile

conditions.other: evaporative formamide-rich films or low-water pools

reactions.mechanism: The aminonitrile amino group is formylated in formamide-rich media, decreasing reversal of the Strecker equilibrium and stabilizing the nitrile intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: N-formylation of aminonitriles in formamide solvent has been shown to protect aminonitriles from back-reaction and thus improve amino-acid-forming trajectories under prebiotic conditions (Green, Xu, & Sutherland, 2021; Green, Xu, & Sutherland, 2023). This branch directly addresses one literature-identified instability issue without claiming to solve branched precursor scarcity.

reactions.name: Biochemical deprotection and hydrolysis of valine aminonitrile pool to valine

conditions.other: prolonged aqueous hydrolysis; aminonitrile half-life less than ~1000 years at 0C and faster at higher temperature

reactions.mechanism: Direct hydrolysis of the nitrile through amide/carboxyl intermediates and hydrolytic deformylation of protected aminonitrile both converge on racemic valine.
reactions.reasoning: Aminonitrile hydrolysis is the canonical irreversible completion step of Strecker amino-acid synthesis, and the report notes hydrolysis timescales under geochemically relevant conditions (Miller, 1998; Miller & Van Trump, 1981). Inclusion of the protected and unprotected pools reflects the stabilization work of Green, Xu, & Sutherland (2021; 2023).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs conversion of isobutyraldehyde to valine hydantoin

conditions.other: CO2/carbonate present; cyanide and ammonia available in concentrated pools

reactions.mechanism: Carbonyl, cyanide, ammonia, and CO2/carbonate chemistry proceeds through hydantoin-forming intermediates rather than free aminonitrile accumulation.
reactions.reasoning: Bucherer-Bergs chemistry is a literature-supported variant linking aldehyde-based cyanide chemistry with hydantoin intermediates under CO2-inclusive conditions and provides an alternative branch to the same amino acid target (Pulletikurti, Yadav, Springsteen, & Krishnamurthy, 2022). This reaction is framed as an auxiliary route, not a replacement for the canonical Strecker pathway.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of valine hydantoin to valine

conditions.other: prolonged hydrolysis in concentrated pools or geothermal cycling

reactions.mechanism: Ring opening and sequential hydrolysis of the hydantoin furnishes the corresponding alpha-amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin intermediates are established precursors to amino acids in Bucherer-Bergs chemistry, though hydrolysis is slower and more condition-sensitive than direct aminonitrile hydrolysis (Pulletikurti et al., 2022). This route is included because the supplied research explicitly identifies it as a bridge between aldehyde/cyanide chemistry and amino-acid formation.

reactions.name: Speculative protometabolic formation of alpha-ketoisovalerate from pyruvate and acetaldehyde inventory

conditions.other: geothermal cycling or mineral-interface protometabolic setting; likely multistep via acetolactate-like intermediates

reactions.mechanism: Carbon-carbon bond formation in a pyruvate-rich protometabolic network yields branched alpha-keto acid inventory, represented here as a compressed net transformation.
reactions.reasoning: The report identifies alpha-ketoisovalerate as a metabolically congruent alternative entry point to valine but also states that its prebiotic formation remains experimentally undemonstrated. This net step is therefore explicitly speculative and included only because it is necessary to connect the documented reductive amination branch to the network. Its logic is motivated by nonenzymatic Krebs-cycle analogs and biosynthetic analogy rather than direct valine-specific demonstration (Muchowska, Varma, & Moran, 2019; Copley, Smith, & Morowitz, 2005).

reactions.name: Reductive amination of alpha-ketoisovalerate to valine on iron/nickel systems

conditions.catalyst: FeS/H2S, freshly precipitated FeS or metallic Ni
conditions.other: alkaline to near-neutral pH; moderate reducing conditions

reactions.mechanism: The alpha-keto acid condenses with ammonia and is reduced to the corresponding alpha-amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-keto acids is a well-supported prebiotic reaction class and provides a biologically congruent route to valine from alpha-ketoisovalerate. FeS/H2S-pyrite-forming systems and freshly precipitated FeS support keto-acid amination, while metallic Ni with H2 at 22C and pH 8 broadens the substrate scope to biological keto acids (Hafenbradl, Keller, Wächtershäuser, & Stetter, 1995; Huber & Wächtershäuser, 2003; Kaur et al., 2024). Barge et al. (2019) further support mineral-mediated reductive amination logic from pyruvate to alanine. Specific prebiotic demonstration for alpha-ketoisovalerate remains indirect, so this branch is plausible but less validated than Strecker chemistry.

reactions.name: Wet-dry clay-mediated peptide formation from valine to valylvaline

conditions.other: wet-dry cycles; ester-mediated condensation favored in concentrated films

reactions.mechanism: Repeated drying promotes activation/ester formation and subsequent amide bond formation between two valine molecules.
reactions.reasoning: Wet-dry cycling is a robust downstream prebiotic mechanism for peptide bond formation. Ester-mediated amide formation under cycling conditions is experimentally supported, and concentrated mineral/salt surfaces enhance oligomerization (Forsythe et al., 2015; Campbell et al., 2019). This reaction is stoichiometrically explicit, avoiding the compression criticized in previous attempts.

pathways.name: Canonical hydrothermal-to-surface Strecker route
pathways.description: Reduced carbon from alkaline hydrothermal chemistry feeds a low-selectivity branched aldehyde pool; concentrated HCN and NH3 in surface ponds convert isobutyraldehyde to valine aminonitrile, which hydrolyzes to valine.

pathways.name: Protected Strecker route via formamide
pathways.description: The canonical Strecker path is supplemented by formamide-mediated N-formylation of the valine aminonitrile, protecting it from reversal before hydrolysis to valine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin route from surface isobutyraldehyde
pathways.description: A CO2-inclusive cyanide/ammonia pathway diverts isobutyraldehyde into valine hydantoin, which then hydrolyzes to valine.

pathways.name: HCN-concentrated short Strecker route
pathways.description: Where HCN is supplied externally by atmospheric/discharge chemistry and concentrated in surface pools, the shortest direct route proceeds through valine aminonitrile to valine.

pathways.name: HCN-formamide-assisted surface Strecker route
pathways.description: An HCN-rich pool first forms a formamide co-solvent, then the valine aminonitrile is protected before final hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Pyruvate-to-alpha-ketoisovalerate reductive amination route
pathways.description: A metabolically congruent but speculative branch converts hydrothermal pyruvate and acetaldehyde inventories into alpha-ketoisovalerate, followed by reductive amination to valine.

pathways.name: Direct pyruvate-rich keto-acid branch
pathways.description: A pyruvate-rich protometabolic pool generates alpha-ketoisovalerate and then valine by iron/nickel-mediated reductive amination.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal reduced-carbon to hydantoin route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reduced carbon feeds surface branched aldehyde formation, but the amino-acid-forming branch proceeds through hydantoin rather than free aminonitrile accumulation.

pathways.name: Surface Strecker route extended to peptide incorporation
pathways.description: Once valine is formed through the canonical aminonitrile route, wet-dry cycling can incorporate it into short peptides such as valylvaline.

pathways.name: Keto-acid route extended to peptide incorporation
pathways.description: A protometabolic keto-acid branch yields valine by reductive amination and then channels it into peptide formation under wet-dry cycling.
